fields.Text:

{{c1::Choriocarcinoma}}

fields.Text: A bone CT reconstruction kernel improves {{c1::edge detail/spatial resolution}} at the cost of increased image noise.

Q2. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Bone kernels enhance edge definition by preserving high spatial frequencies, providing sharp visualization of bone margins and fine detail at the cost of increased noise.

fields.Text: High-energy extremity trauma with numbness and complex fracture/ligamentous injury should prompt {{c1::CTA to evaluate vascular injury}}.

fields.Text:
CT with left ventricular apical aneurysm/thrombus and cerebral infarct. Most likely stroke mechanism?

{{c1::Cardioembolic stroke}}
fields.Extra:
Q4. Correct; difficulty: not listed. The CT images demonstrate marked calcification and thinning of the cardiac apex (blue arrow) with non-enhancement of the hypodense rind which is probably partially related to devascularized myocardium and possibly also partially mural thrombus, which is also hypodense and does not enhance, (red arrow), in keeping with a left ventricular aneurysm.





Open review Q4

fields.Text: Left ventricular aneurysm or mural thrombus can cause {{c1::cardioembolic stroke}}.

fields.Text:
Head CT/MRV pattern with venous-congestion hemorrhage. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Dural venous sinus thrombosis}}

1/4
fields.Extra:
Q5. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: In acute transverse dural sinus thrombosis, the hemorrhage results from venous congestion and impaired drainage rather than arterial rupture, making anticoagulation both safe and necessary to prevent thrombus propagation. The pathophysiology mandates anticoagulation to restore venous outflow and prevent further venous congestion, even in the presence of ventriculostomy catheter.





Open review Q5

fields.Text: Acute dural venous sinus thrombosis is treated with {{c1::anticoagulation}}, even when venous-congestion hemorrhage is present.
fields.Extra:
Q5. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: In acute transverse dural sinus thrombosis, the hemorrhage results from venous congestion and impaired drainage rather than arterial rupture, making anticoagulation both safe and necessary to prevent thrombus propagation. The pathophysiology mandates anticoagulation to restore venous outflow and prevent further venous congestion, even in the presence of ventriculostomy catheter.









Open review Q5

Q8. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Under HIPAA, health information such as diagnostic imaging without any information that can be used to identify a patient (de-identified information) does not constitute protected information.

fields.Text: The MRI control room is typically {{c1::Zone III}}.
fields.Extra:
Q9. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: The MRI control room is located in Zone III, where potential danger exists from interaction between ferromagnetic objects and the magnetic field. Access requires strict restriction under MR personnel supervision with physical barriers such as locks or passkey systems, and personnel must complete MR safety education and screening.

fields.Text: Mammographic sensitivity for breast cancer in extremely dense breast tissue is approximately {{c1::50%}}.
fields.Extra:
Q10. Incorrect; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Mammographic sensitivity drops to approximately 50% in extremely dense breasts due to tissue masking effects, dramatically increasing interval cancer rates and necessitating supplemental screening modalities to maintain effective cancer detection.

fields.Text:
Post-esophagectomy chylous leak treated with lymphangiography and embolic material. Procedure?

{{c1::Thoracic duct embolization}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q15. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: Thoracic duct embolization is a minimally invasive alternative to surgical ligation for chylous leaks. The technique involves pedal lymphangiography followed by transabdominal catheterization and embolization proximal to the leak using coils and liquid embolic agents.

{{c1::Hepatoblastoma}}

1/3

fields.Text: A ring dosimeter is generally worn on the {{c1::dominant hand under gloves with the sensitive area facing the radiation source}}.
fields.Extra:
Q22. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: Ring dosimeters are recommended when hand dose exceeds 50 mSv annually, worn with the sensitive area toward the exposure source. Investigation is triggered at 15 mSv monthly. The dosimeter is typically worn on the dominant hand, which receives the highest exposure during interventional procedures.

fields.Text:
Circumscribed painless palpable breast mass on mammography/US. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Fibroadenoma}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q23. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Fibroadenomas typically appear as oval, circumscribed, isodense masses on mammography and homogeneously hypoechoic masses with circumscribed margins on ultrasound. Characteristic "popcorn-like" calcifications may develop over time in hyalinized fibroadenomas, while complex fibroadenomas can demonstrate suspicious imaging features including irregular margins and internal cystic components requiring biopsy.

fields.Text:
Dysmenorrhea/chronic pelvic pain with thickened junctional zone. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Adenomyosis}}
fields.Extra:
Q26. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: Adenomyosis is characterized by excess endometrial tissue within the myometrium of the uterus. The excess tissue in adenomyosis is confined within the uterus, whereas in endometriosis, the proliferation of endometrial tissue is observed outside the uterus. On MRI, T2 sagittal images of the uterus show an enlarged junctional zone and sub-endometrial cysts.

fields.Text:
Transjugular liver biopsy access route. Preferred hepatic vein?

{{c1::Right hepatic vein}}
fields.Extra:
Q28. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Transjugular liver biopsy (TJLB) is an alternative to a percutaneous liver biopsy in patients with diffuse liver disease, coagulopathy, and ascites. Under ultrasound guidance, the internal jugular vein on the right side is punctured. A guide wire is inserted into the superior vena cava and a sheath is placed over the wire after adequate dilatation. Then a combination of guidewire and a catheter is used to gain entry into the right hepatic vein.




Open review Q28

fields.Text: Transjugular liver biopsy is usually performed through the {{c1::right hepatic vein}}.
fields.Extra:

Under ultrasound guidance, the internal jugular vein on the right side is punctured. A guide wire is inserted into the superior vena cava and a sheath is placed over the wire after adequate dilatation. Then a combination of guidewire and a catheter is used to gain entry into the right hepatic vein.

fields.Text:
Infant with motor delay and absent corpus callosum findings. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Corpus callosum agenesis}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q30. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Agenesis/dysgenesis of the corpus callosum is an anomaly that may occur in isolation or in association with other CNS or systemic malformations. It may be complete, partial, or atypical. With complete agenesis, the corpus callosum is totally absent. In cases of partial agenesis (hypoplasia), the anterior portion (posterior genu and anterior body) is formed, but the posterior portion (posterior body and splenium) is not. The rostrum and the anterior/inferior genu are also absent, depending upon the stage of development at which an insult occurred.




Open review Q30

fields.Text: Corpus callosum agenesis may show {{c1::absent corpus callosum with parallel lateral ventricles/colpocephaly}}.
fields.Extra:
Q30. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Agenesis/dysgenesis of the corpus callosum is an anomaly that may occur in isolation or in association with other CNS or systemic malformations. It may be complete, partial, or atypical. With complete agenesis, the corpus callosum is totally absent. In cases of partial agenesis (hypoplasia), the anterior portion (posterior genu and anterior body) is formed, but the posterior portion (posterior body and splenium) is not. The rostrum and the anterior/inferior genu are also absent, depending upon the stage of development at which an insult occurred.

Ultrasound phantom image with better axial resolution from shorter pulse. Pulse type?

{{c1::Broader bandwidth pulse}}
fields.Extra:

Vital Concept: Broader bandwidth pulses improve axial resolution because they generally are concurrent with shorter temporal and spatial pulse lengths, enabling discrimination of more closely spaced reflectors along the beam axis—the fundamental principle that shorter pulses provide better axial resolution in ultrasound imaging.

{{c1::Facet joint}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q43. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: This entity cannot be reliably distinguished from ganglion cyst on standard MRI. However, communication with the joint space after intra-articular injection with contrast reliably differentiates the two.




Open review Q43

fields.Text: A synovial cyst can be distinguished from a ganglion cyst by showing {{c1::communication with the facet joint after intra-articular contrast injection}}.
fields.Extra:
Q43. Correct; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: This entity cannot be reliably distinguished from ganglion cyst on standard MRI. However, communication with the joint space after intra-articular injection with contrast reliably differentiates the two.

fields.Text: The MQSA average glandular dose limit with a grid is {{c1::3 mGy}} for a 4.2-cm compressed breast of 50/50 glandular-fat composition.
fields.Extra:
Q44. Incorrect; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: The MQSA limits the average glandular dose for a compressed breast thickness of 4.2 cm and a breast composition of 50% glandular and 50% adipose tissue to 3 mGy per film or digital image.

fields.Text:
Gravid uterus with calcified leiomyoma. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Pregnancy with calcified fibroid}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q45. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: First trimester ultrasound is optimal for leiomyoma detection using endovaginal ultrasound. Detection becomes increasingly difficult with advancing gestational age due to fetal growth obscuring myometrial assessment.





Open review Q45

fields.Text: Leiomyomas are easiest to detect in pregnancy during the {{c1::first trimester with endovaginal US}}.
fields.Extra:
Q45. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: First trimester ultrasound is optimal for leiomyoma detection using endovaginal ultrasound. Detection becomes increasingly difficult with advancing gestational age due to fetal growth obscuring myometrial assessment.







Open review Q45

fields.Text:
Geographic hepatic lesion without mass effect. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Focal steatosis/fatty sparing}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q46. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Focal steatosis and fatty sparing tend to be geographic in appearance and often occur in characteristic locations. There is no evidence of mass effect.




Open review Q46

fields.Text: Focal steatosis/fatty sparing is often geographic, occurs in characteristic locations, and shows {{c1::signal change on in- and out-of-phase MRI without mass effect}}.
fields.Extra:
Q46. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Focal steatosis and fatty sparing tend to be geographic in appearance and often occur in characteristic locations. There is no evidence of mass effect.






Open review Q46

fields.Text:
Chest radiograph with incomplete border sign. Most likely lesion location?

{{c1::Pleura/chest wall or pleural-abutting lung}}

1/4
fields.Extra:
Q47. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Incomplete border sign occurs when lesions are only partially visible due to tangential x-ray beam projection - classically seen with pleural plaques but can occur with any pleural-based lesion, chest wall mass, or lung nodule only partially in profile.




Open review Q47

fields.Text: The incomplete border sign localizes a lesion to the {{c1::pleura/chest wall or lung parenchyma abutting the pleura}}.
fields.Extra:
Q47. Correct; difficulty: hard. Vital Concept: Incomplete border sign occurs when lesions are only partially visible due to tangential x-ray beam projection - classically seen with pleural plaques but can occur with any pleural-based lesion, chest wall mass, or lung nodule only partially in profile.








Open review Q47

fields.Text:
Dysphagia with anterolateral cervical esophageal outpouching. Diagnosis?

{{c1::Killian-Jamieson diverticulum}}
fields.Extra:
Q48. Correct; difficulty: moderate. The imaging findings from this esophagram demonstrate a barium-filled outpouching along the anterolateral aspect of the esophagus (blue arrows), which should raise the suggestion of a Killian-Jamieson diverticulum.





Open review Q48

fields.Text: Killian-Jamieson diverticula arise {{c1::anterolaterally just below the cricopharyngeal muscle}}.
fields.Extra:
Q48. Correct; difficulty: moderate. The imaging findings from this esophagram demonstrate a barium-filled outpouching along the anterolateral aspect of the esophagus (blue arrows), which should raise the suggestion of a Killian-Jamieson diverticulum.






Open review Q48

fields.Text:
Adolescent hip pain with widened capital femoral physis. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Slipped capital femoral epiphysis}}
fields.Extra:
Q49. Incorrect; difficulty: not listed. Vital Concept: Widening of the capital femoral physis indicates slipped capital femoral epiphysis, most commonly seen in overweight adolescent boys. Best next step is frog-leg lateral radiograph to assess degree of slippage and the contralateral hip, followed by urgent orthopedic referral for surgical pinning.




Open review Q49

fields.Text: Suspected SCFE on AP pelvis should be evaluated with {{c1::bilateral frog-leg lateral radiographs}}.

Best next step is frog-leg lateral radiograph to assess degree of slippage and the contralateral hip, followed by urgent orthopedic referral for surgical pinning.

fields.Text:
Chest pain, RUQ pain, abdominal distention, and systemic venous congestion. Most likely diagnosis?

{{c1::Congestive heart failure}}

1/5
fields.Extra:
Q50. Correct; difficulty: moderate. This case demonstrates the classic ultrasound signs of passive hepatic congestion: congestion and dilatation of the hepatic veins and IVC, pulsatile flow in the main portal vein, ascites, and gallbladder wall thickening.








Open review Q50

fields.Text: Congestive heart failure can cause {{c1::hepatic congestion, ascites/abdominal distention, and pleural or pulmonary edema findings}}.
fields.Extra:
Q50. Correct; difficulty: moderate. This case demonstrates the classic ultrasound signs of passive hepatic congestion: congestion and dilatation of the hepatic veins and IVC, pulsatile flow in the main portal vein, ascites, and gallbladder wall thickening.

fields.Text: Before I-131 thyroid cancer imaging/therapy, breastfeeding should be stopped for {{c1::at least 6 weeks}} to reduce breast uptake.
fields.Extra:
Q51. Correct; difficulty: hard. Cessation of breastfeeding beginning 6 weeks prior to administration of I-131 is recommended to reduce breast uptake at the time of administration and reduce risk of milk leakage that can negatively impact imaging.




Open review Q51

fields.Text:
Parasymphyseal pubic avulsion injury in an athlete. Tendon/muscle group involved?

{{c1::Adductors}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q52. Correct; difficulty: easy. Vital Concept: Small avulsion fracture from parasymphyseal pubic bone represents muscle attachment injury, commonly involving adductor insertions, typically seen in athletes from sudden forceful muscle contraction during sports activities.




Open review Q52

fields.Text: A parasymphyseal pubic avulsion fracture commonly involves the {{c1::adductor attachments}}.
fields.Extra:

fields.Text:
GI bleeding scan with RLQ uptake increasing like gastric mucosa. Diagnosis/tracer?

{{c1::Meckel diverticulum on Tc-99m pertechnetate}}

1/2
fields.Extra:
Q53. Correct; difficulty: hard. Sequential blood pool images followed by a static planar image reveal a focal area of increased radiotracer activity in right lower quadrant that increases over time, at a rate similar to normal gastric mucosa (curvilinear structure with increased activity in the left abdomen).




Open review Q53

fields.Text: Meckel diverticulum is detected with {{c1::Tc-99m pertechnetate}} because ectopic gastric mucosa accumulates tracer.
fields.Extra:
Q53. Correct; difficulty: hard. Sequential blood pool images followed by a static planar image reveal a focal area of increased radiotracer activity in right lower quadrant that increases over time, at a rate similar to normal gastric mucosa (curvilinear structure with increased activity in the left abdomen).






Open review Q53

fields.Text:
Scrotal fluid collection with septations/debris. Feature supporting pyocele?

{{c1::Internal complexity/septations}}

1/3
fields.Extra:
Q54. Correct; difficulty: moderate. Vital Concept: Internal septations and echogenic debris in scrotal fluid collections represent inflammatory organization and infectious debris—the key ultrasound feature that transforms a simple hydrocele into pyocele, fundamentally changing clinical management from observation to active antimicrobial treatment.




Open review Q54

fields.Text: A pyocele is suggested by a scrotal fluid collection with {{c1::internal septations and echogenic debris}}.

{{c1::Endometrial neoplasia requiring biopsy}}
